campaigns.title: Help abandoned grandparents fight hunger and receive medical care. Donate now
campaigns.description: 65-year-old Shakuntala completely depends on our partner NGO, Share and Care, for her food requirements, as she has no one else to take care of her. “When my only child was young, my husband Nadesan died. It was just me and my young son. After my son got married, he left me to stay with his in-laws, and I was alone. I am old, have pain in my legs, knees, shoulders and my entire body so I cannot work as I once did. But the food rations such as the nuts, lentils, wheat flour and rice I receive from Share and Care help me tremendously and I can eat well every day,” she says. Share and Care helps hundreds of abandoned grandparents, most of them being elderly women. The senior citizens belong to tribal communities and have limited access to necessities such as food and medicines to stay alive and well. This Shivratri,  you can help with food and other essentials. Support them. Donate now. Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India), & 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code (USA). Abandoned elderly supported by Share and Care400 elderly parents abandoned by their children have no one to take care of them and need your help. They suffer from hunger, sight and hearing failure, diabetes, high blood pressure and other medical conditions. As they cannot work and support themselves because of old age, they cannot afford medicines or even clothing. So, they remain poor and alone. Share and Care’s mission Since 1984, Share and Care has been caring for disadvantaged groups of people, including elderly parents who have been living in poverty after being abandoned by their children. The founders, a couple named Carmel and Arokiasamy, aim to eliminate poverty and the suffering of the poor through social welfare programs and education. To achieve this mission, Share and Care runs a program in Chennai and also a village in Tiruvallur, where the 400 elderly live. This program ensures that every month, the 400 senior citizens have dry food rations, medicines and medical care, clothes, physiotherapy, geriatric counselling and other services to nurture them back to good physical health. Need for the fundraiserOne of the founders, Carmel Arokiasamy, shares that the abandoned elderly parents who live in extreme poverty without food or anyone to care for them constantly think about their children and ask, “Did we do something wrong?” Arokiasamy shares that from the time their children are born, they take care of them in such a special way. But when their children abandon them, they weep and cannot stop thinking about them. While dealing with this emotional distress, they are also suffering from hunger and disease. But cannot afford food and medicines. Donate now to help Share and Care provide the abandoned elderly with dry food rations, medical supplies, a medical and eye camp, counselling, clothes, blankets and recreational activities to live their lives with a positive outlook, despite their circumstances.Your donation can provide these elderly parents with their most basic and urgent needs. “Please support our work because this is for the elderly parents who are in great need. These grandparents are bedridden. They need care, love and support”.Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s largest online donation platform, to support Share and Care in their mission to provide food, medicines, counselling and personal care to 400 abandoned grandparents living in poverty.

campaigns.title: For tribal children of Majuli Island, education is the only way out. Help Bipin ensure they go to school
campaigns.description: “The Hummingbird School is my only hope to succeed in life. I feel I am trying to swim out of this place (Majuli) and my school stands as a beacon on the other side of the river. If my school closes down, I feel I will drown in this river. Not just me but every child in villages around here and our families depend on this school”, says Roopjyoti, a bright, young student of Ayang Trust’s The Hummingbird School.Majuli Island, where The Hummingbird School is situated, faces severe floods and constant riverbank erosion, leaving most of its residents struggling to survive. For the children of this impoverished tribal community, education remains their only path to hope and dignity.Your support to Ayang Trust’s Hummingbird School can ensure that its students can have a future different from their parents who are struggling to get even two meals a day. Donate now.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).From IIT Kharagpur to Majuli“When I first visited the remote villages of Majuli I was shocked. The extent of the struggles here was beyond my imagination. These villages had no connectivity to the outside world, and very limited access to healthcare and quality education”, says Bipin Dhane, founder of the school. Bipin, who grew up in the small town of Satara in Maharashtra, faced countless financial hardships. Despite his struggles, Bipin secured admission to the prestigious Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, and then went on to take up a job in Singapore. Owing to his childhood experiences Bipin was always inclined towards helping the less fortunate, and he believed in the transformative power of education. It was in 2016 that Bipin left his lucrative career abroad and moved to Majuli, to provide the children from this inaccessible river island education they were deprived of.“Whatever little money I had saved, I gave it to my parents. I had no money to start the school. It was then that villagers came together to build bamboo huts. Each family donated bamboo, wood, and whatever little they could. It was the parents of our students who erected huts for the school to start”, shares Bipin while talking about the inception of The Hummingbird School in 2017.Help Bipin save underprivileged children of Majuli Island from generational poverty and give them a chance to succeed. Donate now.Donate to save future generations“People of the island value education greatly because they know that their children have no future without it. Parents have vested their faith in The Humming Bird School because they don't want their children to suffer the way they have been”, says Bipin. Bipin says, “Today we have over 300 students in our school and about 90 children live in our hostel. Many of these children are orphaned or come from single-parent households, struggling each day without adequate care or resources.. I urge each one of you to come forward and donate to us so we can help hundreds of children escape from the cycle of poverty and build lives of dignity, hope, and opportunity.”Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s largest and most trusted online donation platform, to help children of Majuli Island get an education and break the cycle of poverty.

campaigns.title: Help Mansi bring hope into lives of children fighting for life, Donate now
campaigns.description: When even hospitals and doctors gave up on children fighting for life Mansi Shah stood by them. Mansi founded Happy Feet Home, an NGO that cares for children fighting severe illnesses like thalassemia, HIV, Cancer, etc. Despite being discouraged repeatedly, she refused to turn away from children who were often overlooked simply because their journeys were difficult and uncertain. Today, Happy Feet Home provides children with medical care, nutritious food, education, emotional support and, above all, the love and dignity every child deserves.Your donation can help ensure these children continue receiving lifelong care. Donate today and give them the chance to live with hope.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking.Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).Born from one unforgettable momentHappy Feet Home was born from an experience Mansi could never forget. "I was very attached to this 3-year-old girl. She had a brain tumour," she says. When the child hugged her tightly before boarding a train home, it felt like a final goodbye. That heartbreaking moment inspired Mansi to dedicate her life to children who may never receive a cure. "If I start something, I will work with children who have illnesses which may not have a cure... That's when the idea of Happy Feet Home came into my mind."Every donation changes a child's futureMany children arrive at Happy Feet Home carrying far more than an illness. "It's not 1, not 2, but 3 or 4 issues that they are grappling with in life," Mansi explains. Poverty, abandonment and years without affection often leave emotional scars as deep as their medical conditions. Some children have even told her that the first hug they ever received was after arriving at the centre.Yet with proper nutrition, medicines, counselling and constant care, lives begin to change. One girl who was once not responding to treatment is now a healthy college student. Another boy arrived weighing just 17 kilos after living on the streets. Today, he is healthy and learning professional cricket. These transformations are only possible because compassionate people chose to donate.Why your support is urgently neededProviding lifelong care for children with HIV, thalassemia and cancer requires constant support. There was even a time when Happy Feet Home had to shut its centre for nearly two weeks because of a lack of funding. During that period, children lost access to nutrition, education and mental health support. As Mansi explains, "Illnesses like HIV and Thalassemia are incurable. The need for medicine, for treatment, for care and support is going to be a forever need."That is why your donation matters today.Donate to Happy Feet HomeEvery donation helps provide medicines, nutritious meals, education, counselling and a loving home for children fighting serious illnesses. As Mansi beautifully puts it, "As a donor, you are not only giving money. You are ensuring that children with illnesses have the right to live as children. You are giving them hope."If you believe no child should be denied love, dignity or happiness because of illness, please support this NGO for children today. "If you believe that children with HIV, Thalassemia, Cancer have a right to live a dignified life please support our cause and donate today."Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s most trusted online donation platform, to support Happy Feet Home in its mission to provide care, dignity, medical support, and hope to children living with life-limiting and life-shortening illnesses.

campaigns.title: Help Leena in saving girls from being lost forever in red-light areas
campaigns.description: "Hundreds of young girls are lost forever in the dark lanes of red-light areas," says Leena Kejriwal, the founder of Missing Link Trust. These girls endure unimaginable abuse and exploitation, being lured or trafficked into an industry that breaks them physically and emotionally. Missing Link Trust is on a mission to prevent the tragedy before it happens, and also rehabilitate girls and women who are rescued from forced sex work.Leena’s journey began with her powerful street art project in Kolkata, where she painted haunting silhouettes of missing girls to raise awareness against sex trafficking. Now, she leads a larger fight — one that focuses on prevention and rehabilitation. But this mission needs your support.You can save countless girl children from going missing forever. Donate now.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).Preventing Trafficking Before It Begins“When girls come into a red-light area, the first thing is that they are broken, physically and emotionally,” Leena says. “It’s like they will be caught, hands and legs tied, and all heinous crimes committed on them.” These are the brutal realities that Missing Link Trust fights against every day.Many of these girls are from impoverished regions like the Sundarbans, and traffickers prey on their vulnerability. “It’s shocking that they sell themselves for as low as a hundred rupees,” Leena adds. "They ready her through violent acts, removing all threads of shame from her."But why wait until a girl is trafficked to save her? Missing Link Trust believes in stopping trafficking at its root — through “prevention, prevention, prevention” as Leena puts it. The goal is simple: protect girls from ever being lost to this darkness.A Safe World for Children“Our mission is the prevention of abuse and exploitation of children,” Leena emphasizes. “Our vision is to create a safe world for children, and we want to do this with a preventive methodology.”Every Donation MattersYour support will help Missing Link Trust continue its vital work of rescuing, rehabilitating, and preventing the trafficking of young girls. The funds raised will go directly towards:Prevention programs in vulnerable areas to stop trafficking before it happens.Rehabilitation services for girls rescued from red-light areas, providing them with medical care, emotional support, and education.Awareness campaigns to reach at-risk communities and ensure girls are protected from traffickers.Together, We Can Make a Difference“Why wait for a girl to get trafficked to save her?” asks Leena. With your help, we can stop this horror before it begins. Your donation will help us create a world where no girl is lost to the nightmare of trafficking.Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s largest and most trusted online giving platform, to support Missing Link Trust to save young girls from being trafficked and help rehabilitate those rescued.

campaigns.title: Help feed and care for elderly parents abandoned by their own families
campaigns.description: Vijaya was around 70 years old and a street in Avadi, Chennai was her home. Abandoned by family, she had no roof to call home and no means of survival. The police had warned her to move on. Residents had complained. Nobody wanted to touch what they saw as somebody else's problem. Then someone called Anbagam. The rescue team from the NGO arrived. There was a complication: Vijaya refused to leave without her puppies. The team found homes for each one. Then they brought Vijaya in. Today she is healthy, comfortable, and happy. This is what Anbagam does. It meets people exactly where they are, asks nothing of them except to come, and gives them something most of us take entirely for granted: a safe place to be.But without your support this safe haven for the elderly might not even exist. Be the pillar of support this old age home needs. Donate generously!Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).A life-long missionSince 1999, Mohammed Rafi, a small businessman from Parry's in Chennai, has been doing this work. He started by housing people in his own home. When that filled up, he built more. Today, Anbagam runs three homes across Chennai and Thiruvallur, caring for 400 residents: mentally ill, elderly, abandoned, forgotten.Running this for 400 people costs money. Food alone costs roughly Rs 4 lakh every month. Medicines and psychiatric care cost approximately Rs 1 lakh a month. Add staff, a doctor, cleaning, bedding, and clothing, and the annual operating cost across all three homes comes to around Rs 77 lakh.Your contribution means a warm meal, a comfortable bed and necessary medical treatment for an abandoned elderly grandparent. Support Now!Impact beyond numbersOver 7,000 people have walked through Anbagam's doors. Around 3,000 have been reunited with families across India. The rest stay, because Anbagam is the family they have now.Mohammed Rafi said it simply: “Share your happiness with the people who are here. Help them!”Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s most trusted online donation platform, to support Anbagam, in its mission to give abandoned elderly parents the care they deserve.

campaigns.title: Help feed devotees during Puri Jagannath Rath Yatra from  July 16-24
campaigns.description: Every year, the sacred Jagannath Rath Yatra draws millions to witness Lord Jagannath's chariot procession. Among them are thousands of elderly and other devotees who travel from remote villages, carrying little beyond their faith. What they don't carry is food.The Yatra stretches across hours, often through heavy monsoon rain. Pilgrims stand for long periods, pressed into crowds, waiting for a glimpse of the deities. Many have no money left for a meal after the cost of travel.Pure Devotion Foundation (PDF) stands in this gap.Through its Yatra meal programme, the PDF serves warm, simple meals to devotees at points along the pilgrimage route, food handed over with the same reverence the devotees bring to their prayers. For many of these devotees, this meal is the only one they will eat that day. The need grows every year, as the crowds do. Volunteers on the ground report the same scene repeatedly: elderly men and women, drenched from the rain, sitting on the roadside because they can no longer stand — some too proud to ask for help, some too weak to. PDF prepares meals in bulk, transports to the route, and serves fresh through the duration of the Yatra, regardless of rain, crowd density, or logistical hurdles.You can be the reason a devotee doesn't go hungry this Yatra season.A contribution, however modest, helps Pure Devotion Foundation prepare and serve one more meal, reach one more devotee.Give.do is India's largest and most trusted online donation platform, connecting donors directly to verified NGOs like Pure Devotion Foundation.This Rath Yatra, help ensure that faith isn't met with hunger. Donate today, and let no devotee go without a meal on their sacred journey.

campaigns.title: Help Educate Slum Kids and Support the Livelihood of Poor Women in Sharfabad, Noida
campaigns.description:
Various cities across India, especially metropolis face a difficult situation where many financially deprived kids living in slums are deprived of education. They either can't afford a basic quality education or drop out of school because of financial pressure. Many children are pushed into professional begging and rag-picking. Similarly, women coming from slums do not have either employment opportunities or essential skills to earn a livelihood to support their families. Even though their financial situation is tough, they are helpless. Our project serves the populous slum neighborhood of Noida especially Sarfabad with a total population of 11,078 which comprises 5000 women. 
 Indian Centre for Development and Rights comes up with the project 'Ashraya' emphasizing child protection, systematic education, and women empowerment. This Project is to minimize the dropouts from schools, help them connect to mainstream education through creative learning, aid their education by providing them scholarships and make education a fun learning experience. Ashraya empowers women through skill training and livelihood generation making them self-dependent and providing counseling. We strive forward to put a strong impact on the localities of the project making them financially independent and improving their overall economics setting up a standard for quality education and fanning their talents and skills, making them follow their passion, and helping them monetize their skills through various activities. We extend support to young lads and kids through regular academic classes, art, and crafts, music and dance, spoken English, value education, computer training, and various other ways of innovative learning. Our women empowerment plan includes health and sanitation awareness, basic education, providing Training and certificated courses on Stitching, knitting, makeup, parlor training, etc, and helping them set up small businesses of Dhupa Bati, Pickle Papad, Madhubani painting, handicrafts, etc. 
 We at ICDFR strive to achieve a happy and healthy world, where people live in harmony and a sustainable environment for generations to come. We believe it is possible to make a world that knows no suffering and that knowledge permeates all levels. Improving the education system, providing basic healthcare facilities, raising awareness of human rights, empowering women to increase gender equality, as well as focus on environmental sustainability in rural India, are the primary goals of our organization.

campaigns.title: Help abandoned elderly with shelter and care
campaigns.description: Every day, elderly parents are left alone on the streets, at train stations, or even outside temples. Many are sick, struggling with age-related problems, and have no money or family to turn to. Some suffer for weeks or months before anyone notices their pain. The heartbreaking truth? They were abandoned by their own families.Although Indian law requires children and relatives to care for senior citizens, many elders still face neglect, abuse, and abandonment. The problem is only getting worse.But there is hope. Our dedicated NGO partners work day and night to rescue these forgotten elders. They provide safe shelter, medical care, nutritious meals, and most importantly—love and respect. Through old age homes across the country, our partners give these seniors a chance to live their remaining years with dignity, comfort, and peace.You can make a difference. Your donation will help our NGO partners:Rescue homeless elders from the streetsProvide shelter, food, and medical careRestore their faith in humanity with kindness and supportNo elder should spend their final years alone and suffering. Your generosity can give them a fresh start. Donate today and help an abandoned senior citizen find safety, care, and hope.

Pledge your support, donate now!

campaigns.title: Help Save Young Girls From Trafficking
campaigns.description: Human trafficking in India is a horrifying crisis. Thousands of victims, primarily women and girl children, are exploited in red-light areas. Over 10,659 cases were reported between 2018-2022, but experts say that these numbers are likely a fraction of the reality due to underreporting, yet they still do expose the magnitude of the crisis. Girls make up 71.4% of missing children in India and a large number of these girls end up at red-light areas. Sex trafficking is particularly gruesome, with girls as young as 12 sold for as little as a few thousand rupees.
